conditions.title: Academic experience

criteria.text: Without prejudice to the principles and requirements provided for by any other condition of registration and the scope of B1.1, the provider must ensure that the students registered on each higher education course receive a high quality academic experience.
criteria.threshold: Students receive a high quality academic experience on each course
risk_indicators: Student complaints about course quality or academic delivery
External examiner reports highlighting concerns about academic standards
Poor student satisfaction scores in academic experience metrics
High dropout rates or academic appeals

criteria.text: For the purposes of this condition, a high quality academic experience includes but is not limited to ensuring all of the following: a. each higher education course is up-to-date; b. each higher education course provides educational challenge; c. each higher education course is coherent; d. each higher education course is effectively delivered; and e. each higher education course, as appropriate to the subject matter of the course, requires students to develop relevant skills.
criteria.threshold: Courses are up-to-date, challenging, coherent, effectively delivered, and develop relevant skills
risk_indicators: Curriculum not updated for several years or lacking current industry relevance
Student feedback indicating courses are too easy or lack intellectual challenge
Disjointed course structure with poor module sequencing or unclear learning progression
Skills assessments showing graduates lack competencies expected by employers

criteria.text: Insofar as relevant skills includes technical proficiency in the English language, the provider is not required to comply with B1.3.e to the extent that it is able to demonstrate to the OfS, on the balance of probabilities, that its English language proficiency requirements, or failure to have English language proficiency requirements, for one or more students, are strictly necessary as a matter of law because compliance with B1.3.e in respect of that student, or those students: i. would amount to a form of discrimination for the purposes of the Equality Act 2010; and ii. cannot be objectively justified for the purposes of relevant provisions of that Act; and iii. does not fall within an exception or exclusion provided for under or by virtue of that Act, including but not limited to provisions of the Act that relate to competence standards.
criteria.threshold: Demonstrate legal necessity for any exemption from English language proficiency requirements under Equality Act 2010
risk_indicators: Blanket exemptions from English language requirements without proper legal justification
Students struggling academically due to language barriers without appropriate support
Failure to document legal basis for exemptions from English proficiency requirements
Discrimination complaints related to language requirements or exemptions

criteria.text: "appropriately informed" will be assessed by reference to: i. the time period within which any of the developments described in the definition of up-to-date have been in existence; ii. the importance of any of the developments described in the definition of up-to-date to the subject matter of the higher education course; and iii. the time period by which it is planned that such developments described in the definition of up-to-date will be brought into the higher education course content.
criteria.threshold: Course content must be informed by developments according to their recency, importance to the subject, and planned integration timeline
risk_indicators: Course content references outdated practices or research significantly behind current developments
No clear timeline or process for integrating recent developments into curriculum
Critical industry or professional developments not reflected in course materials
Extended delays between relevant developments and their incorporation into teaching

criteria.text: "coherent" means a higher education course which ensures: i. there is an appropriate balance between breadth and depth of content; ii. subjects and skills are taught in an appropriate order and, where necessary, build on each other throughout the course; and iii. key concepts are introduced at the appropriate point in the course content.
criteria.threshold: Course structure must demonstrate logical progression with balanced breadth/depth and appropriate sequencing of concepts
risk_indicators: Students struggling with concepts due to missing prerequisite knowledge
Curriculum mapping reveals gaps or inappropriate sequencing of topics
Unbalanced course content that is either too superficial across all areas or excessively narrow
Key foundational concepts introduced too late or advanced concepts presented without proper preparation

criteria.text: "educational challenge" means a challenge that is no less than the minimum level of rigour and difficulty reasonably expected of the higher education course, in the context of the subject matter and level of the course.
criteria.threshold: Course must provide appropriate level of academic rigour and difficulty for its subject and level
risk_indicators: Assessment tasks consistently completed with minimal effort or preparation
Grade inflation patterns suggesting insufficient academic challenge
Employer or professional body feedback indicating graduates lack expected competency levels
Comparative analysis showing significantly lower difficulty than equivalent courses elsewhere

criteria.text: "effectively delivered", in relation to a higher education course, means the manner in which it is taught, supervised and assessed (both in person and remotely) including, but not limited to, ensuring: i. an appropriate balance between delivery methods, for example lectures, seminars, group work or practical study, as relevant to the content of the course; and ii. an appropriate balance between directed and independent study or research, as relevant to the level of the course.
criteria.threshold: Teaching delivery must use appropriate mix of methods and balance directed/independent learning for the course level
risk_indicators: Over-reliance on single delivery method inappropriate for course content or level
Student feedback indicating poor balance between contact time and independent study
Learning outcomes not achieved due to inappropriate delivery methods
Supervision arrangements inadequate for supporting student progress and achievement

criteria.text: The reference to higher education provided "in any manner or form" includes any higher education course (whether or not that course is recognised for OfS funding purposes, or any other purpose), at any level, and with any volume of learning. This means, for example, that postgraduate research courses, the study of modules or courses leading to microcredentials, and apprenticeships are included within the scope of this condition. It also includes courses provided face-to-face, by distance learning, or a combination of delivery approaches.
criteria.threshold: All forms of higher education provision must be covered regardless of format, level, or delivery method
risk_indicators: Institution excludes certain course types from quality assurance processes
Different standards applied to online versus face-to-face delivery
Microcredentials or apprenticeships treated as outside regulatory scope
Postgraduate research provision managed separately from institutional oversight

criteria.text: This condition applies to any higher education provided "by, or on behalf of, a provider". This includes higher education provided to all of the students who are registered with a registered provider, taught by a registered provider or studying for an award of a registered provider (or where these services are provided on a registered provider's behalf). This includes UK-based and non-UK-based students, and courses delivered through partnership arrangements both within the UK and internationally.
criteria.threshold: Responsibility extends to all students and courses connected to the provider, including international partnerships
risk_indicators: Overseas partnership arrangements lack adequate oversight
Different quality standards applied to international versus domestic students
Partnership agreements unclear about regulatory responsibilities
Students studying remotely from abroad receive diminished support

criteria.text: The reference to "including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider is responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider" means that a provider is required to comply with the provisions of this condition where it is the awarding body for a course, whether or not that provider has any other role in the design or delivery of that course.
criteria.threshold: Awarding bodies must ensure compliance even when only responsible for granting awards
risk_indicators: Limited oversight of courses where provider only awards but doesn't deliver
Inadequate quality assurance of partner-delivered programmes
Unclear accountability arrangements with teaching partners
Awards granted without sufficient evidence of learning outcomes achievement

criteria.text: Where a provider is not the awarding body for a course, this condition applies to a course the provider itself delivers, or which is delivered on its behalf, regardless of the identity of the awarding body, whether or not that awarding body is registered with the OfS, or the nature of any partnership agreement. For the avoidance of doubt, this means for example, that a provider delivering, or allowing another provider to deliver, courses leading to a qualification awarded by Pearson is responsible for compliance with this condition in relation to those courses. Similarly, a provider delivering, or allowing another provider to deliver, courses leading to a qualification awarded by another higher education provider, whether that awarding provider is located in England or elsewhere, is responsible for compliance with this condition in relation to those courses.
criteria.threshold: Teaching providers must ensure compliance regardless of who awards the qualification
risk_indicators: Courses delivered under external awarding arrangements lack internal quality oversight
Different compliance standards applied based on awarding body identity
Teaching delivery not aligned with institutional quality frameworks
Responsibility boundaries unclear in multi-provider arrangements

criteria.text: In practice, these provisions may result in more than one registered provider being responsible for compliance with this condition in relation to the same course.
criteria.threshold: Multiple providers may share responsibility for the same course compliance
risk_indicators: Unclear division of responsibilities between multiple providers
Gaps in oversight due to shared responsibility assumptions
Conflicting quality assurance requirements from different responsible providers
Students receive inconsistent experience due to multiple provider involvement

criteria.text: The subject matter of this condition includes matters that relate to the curriculum and pedagogy for higher education courses. The OfS would expect to draw on expert academic judgement, including from subject experts, before reaching a view that the condition was not satisfied in relation to such matters.
criteria.threshold: Curriculum and pedagogy matters must meet standards as assessed through expert academic judgement
risk_indicators: Curriculum content or structure that significantly deviates from sector norms without justification
Pedagogical approaches that are demonstrably ineffective or inappropriate for the subject level
External examiner reports consistently raising concerns about curriculum design or teaching methods
Student feedback indicating systematic issues with course content or delivery methods

criteria.text: The course content, including topics and reading lists, must be informed by research and scholarship and reflect professional developments, including the adoption of the latest professional or industry standards.
criteria.threshold: Course content is current and representative of rigorous academic thinking
risk_indicators: Course content or reading lists have not been updated for multiple years
Course materials do not reflect current professional or industry standards
Content does not incorporate recent research developments in the field
Professional, statutory or regulatory body requirements are not met

criteria.text: The pedagogy of a course must be representative of current thinking and practices, including appropriate pedagogy for digital delivery where courses are delivered wholly or in part online.
criteria.threshold: Teaching methods are current and appropriate to delivery mode
risk_indicators: Online courses use inappropriate or outdated digital pedagogy
Teaching methods do not reflect current educational practices
Pedagogy has not evolved with technological or methodological advances
Delivery methods are misaligned with course content or student needs

criteria.text: Courses must provide appropriate educational challenge, with sufficient rigour and difficulty appropriate to the level and stage of study.
criteria.threshold: Educational challenge is appropriate to qualification level
risk_indicators: Integrated masters final year lacks sufficient rigour compared to earlier stages
Foundation years do not adequately prepare students for subsequent study
Research questions provide limited scope for original ideas
Challenge level is inappropriate for the qualification being awarded

criteria.text: Course content must be coherent, with appropriate breadth and depth, logical sequencing of learning, and module choices that enable coherent pathways.
criteria.threshold: Course structure supports coherent learning progression
risk_indicators: Course content is too narrow or too broad for the qualification
Students lack security in foundational topics before advanced study
Practical components are disconnected from related theory
Module choices do not result in coherent learning experiences

criteria.text: Courses must be effectively delivered through appropriate range of learning activities, effective delivery methods, supportive research environments, and integrated assessment.
criteria.threshold: Delivery methods effectively support student learning
risk_indicators: Over-reliance on large-scale lectures without small group teaching
Insufficient opportunities for direct engagement with teaching staff
Inadequate supervision or research environment support
Assessment is poorly integrated or feedback is insufficient or untimely

criteria.text: Courses must enable students to develop and demonstrate relevant intellectual and professional skills consistent with the subject and level of study.
criteria.threshold: Students develop skills appropriate to their field and qualification level
risk_indicators: Courses do not develop essential intellectual skills like critical evaluation
Professional courses lack required skills for relevant career paths
Skills development is not aligned with PSRB requirements where applicable
Skills teaching is poorly timed or inadequately integrated

criteria.text: In order to successfully rely on the exception in B1.4, the nature of the evidence a provider would need to put forward would go beyond articulating potential legal concerns or matters to which it has had regard in its decision-making and would require compelling evidence and reasoning on matters of law.
criteria.threshold: Compelling evidence and reasoning on matters of law must be provided
risk_indicators: Provider citing B1.4 exception without providing legal evidence or reasoning
General statements about legal concerns without specific legal analysis
Failure to provide compelling evidence when claiming Equality Act conflicts
Vague references to legal considerations without substantive legal reasoning

criteria.text: As the exception in B1.4 only applies 'to the extent' that a provider can demonstrate a conflict with the Equality Act 2010, it would not be sufficient for a provider to put forward evidence and reasoning about its requirements (or absence of requirements) in general terms; rather, a provider would need to address the particular requirements (or absence of requirements) which it is seeking to justify under the exception, and the particular courses to which these relate.
criteria.threshold: Specific evidence required for particular requirements and courses, not general terms
risk_indicators: Provider making broad claims about Equality Act conflicts without course-specific evidence
General policy statements without reference to specific requirements or courses
Failure to identify particular requirements being justified under the exception
Non-specific reasoning that doesn't address individual course contexts

criteria.text: The OfS expects that potential conflicts between requirements relating to English language proficiency and the Equality Act 2010 will only arise in rare cases, and therefore that this exception is likely to be invoked only in limited circumstances, for example in relation to students with particular learning disabilities.
criteria.threshold: Exception should only be invoked in rare, limited circumstances with specific justification
risk_indicators: Frequent or routine invocation of B1.4 exception across multiple courses
Use of exception for general English language policy rather than specific disability accommodations
Broad application of exception beyond rare cases involving particular learning disabilities
Pattern of using exception as standard practice rather than exceptional circumstances

conditions.notes: This condition applies to the quality of higher education provided in any manner or form by, or on behalf of, a provider (including circumstances where a provider is responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider). The condition includes specific provisions regarding English language proficiency requirements and exemptions under the Equality Act 2010.

conditions.title: Resources, support and student engagement

criteria.text: This condition applies to the quality of higher education provided in any manner or form by, or on behalf of, a provider (including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider is responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider).
criteria.threshold: Universal application to all forms of higher education provision
risk_indicators: Provider attempting to exclude certain types of provision from quality requirements
Inconsistent application of resources and support across different delivery modes
Quality standards varying between direct provision and partnership arrangements

criteria.text: each cohort of students registered on each higher education course receives resources and support which are sufficient for the purpose of ensuring: i. a high quality academic experience for those students; and ii. those students succeed in and beyond higher education
criteria.threshold: Sufficient resources and support for high quality experience and student success across all cohorts
risk_indicators: Student complaints about inadequate library, IT, or learning resources
Declining student satisfaction scores in resource-related areas
Significant disparities in resource allocation between different student cohorts
Poor graduate outcomes or employment rates indicating insufficient preparation

criteria.text: effective engagement with each cohort of students which is sufficient for the purpose of ensuring: i. a high quality academic experience for those students; and ii. those students succeed in and beyond higher education
criteria.threshold: Effective student engagement sufficient for quality experience and success outcomes
risk_indicators: Low student attendance or participation rates in learning activities
Minimal or ineffective student feedback mechanisms
Poor student-staff ratios affecting meaningful engagement opportunities
High dropout rates or academic failure rates indicating insufficient engagement

criteria.text: For the purposes of this condition, "all reasonable steps" is to be interpreted in a manner which (without prejudice to other relevant considerations): a. focuses and places significant weight on: i. the particular academic needs of each cohort of students based on prior academic attainment and capability; and ii. the principle that the greater the academic needs of the cohort of students, the number and nature of the steps needed to be taken are likely to be more significant; b. places less weight, as compared to the factor described in B2.3a., on the provider's financial constraints; and c. disregards case law relating to the interpretation of contractual obligations.
criteria.threshold: Reasonable steps prioritizing student academic needs over financial constraints
risk_indicators: Provider using financial constraints as primary justification for inadequate support
Uniform support provision without consideration of varying student academic needs
Evidence of reduced support for cohorts with greater academic needs
Reliance on contractual minimums rather than student-centered provision

criteria.text: academic misconduct means any action or attempted action that may result in a student obtaining an unfair academic advantage in relation to an assessment, including but not limited to plagiarism, unauthorised collaboration and the possession of unauthorised materials during an assessment.
criteria.threshold: Clear definition and understanding of academic misconduct established
risk_indicators: High rates of academic misconduct cases reported
Unclear or inconsistent academic integrity policies
Students report confusion about what constitutes academic misconduct
Weak detection systems for plagiarism and unauthorised collaboration

criteria.text: appropriately qualified means staff have and maintain: i. expert knowledge of the subject they design and/or deliver; ii. teaching qualifications or training, and teaching experience, appropriate for the content and level of the relevant higher education course; and iii. the required knowledge and skills as to the effective delivery of their higher education course.
criteria.threshold: All teaching staff meet qualification requirements and maintain expertise
risk_indicators: Staff teaching outside their area of expertise
High proportion of staff without relevant teaching qualifications
Lack of professional development opportunities for staff
Student feedback indicating poor teaching quality

criteria.text: sufficient in number will be assessed by reference to the principle that the larger the cohort size of students, the greater the number of staff and amount of staff time should be available to students, and means, in the context of the staff team: i. there is sufficient financial resource to recruit and retain sufficient staff; ii. the provider allocates appropriate financial resource to ensuring staff are equipped to teach courses; iii. higher education courses have an adequate number of staff, and amount of staff time; and iv. the impact on students of changes in staffing is minimal.
criteria.threshold: Adequate staffing levels maintained proportionate to student cohort size
risk_indicators: High student-to-staff ratios compared to sector benchmarks
Frequent staff turnover or recruitment difficulties
Students reporting limited access to teaching staff
Courses experiencing significant staffing disruptions

criteria.text: resources includes but is not limited to: i. the staff team that designs and delivers a higher education course being collectively sufficient in number, appropriately qualified and deployed effectively to deliver in practice; and ii. physical and digital learning resources that are adequate and deployed effectively to meet the needs of the cohort of students.
criteria.threshold: Adequate physical, digital and human resources deployed effectively
risk_indicators: Student complaints about inadequate learning facilities or equipment
Outdated or insufficient digital learning platforms
Library and laboratory resources below sector standards
Poor utilisation of available resources

criteria.text: The reference to higher education provided "in any manner or form" includes any higher education course (whether or not that course is recognised for OfS funding purposes, or any other purpose), at any level, and with any volume of learning. This means, for example, that postgraduate research courses, the study of modules or courses leading to microcredentials, and apprenticeships are included within the scope of this condition. It also includes courses provided face-to-face, by distance learning, or a combination of delivery approaches.
criteria.threshold: All higher education provision regardless of format, level, or delivery method must be covered
risk_indicators: Exclusion of certain course types from quality assurance processes
Different standards applied to distance learning vs face-to-face provision
Microcredentials or apprenticeships not subject to institutional oversight
Failure to monitor quality across all delivery modes

criteria.text: This condition applies to any higher education provided "by, or on behalf of, a provider". This includes higher education provided to all of the students who are registered with a registered provider, taught by a registered provider or studying for an award of a registered provider (or where these services are provided on a registered provider's behalf). This includes UK-based and non-UK-based students, and courses delivered through partnership arrangements both within the UK and internationally.
criteria.threshold: All students and partnership arrangements must be covered regardless of location or delivery method
risk_indicators: Inconsistent quality standards between UK and international partnerships
Different treatment of overseas students in quality processes
Partnership agreements lacking adequate quality oversight clauses
Failure to monitor third-party delivery arrangements

criteria.text: The reference to "including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider is responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider" means that a provider is required to comply with the provisions of this condition where it is the awarding body for a course, whether or not that provider has any other role in the design or delivery of that course.
criteria.threshold: Awarding bodies must ensure compliance even when not involved in course design or delivery
risk_indicators: Inadequate oversight of courses where provider is awarding body only
Lack of quality assurance processes for externally delivered programmes
Insufficient monitoring of teaching providers delivering awards
Weak governance arrangements for award-only relationships

criteria.text: Where a provider is not the awarding body for a course, this condition applies to a course the provider itself delivers, or which is delivered on its behalf, regardless of the identity of the awarding body, whether or not that awarding body is registered with the OfS, or the nature of any partnership agreement.
criteria.threshold: Teaching providers must ensure compliance regardless of awarding body identity or registration status
risk_indicators: Lower quality standards applied to courses with external awards
Inadequate quality processes for non-OfS registered awarding bodies
Assumption that external awarding body responsibility removes provider obligations
Weak quality assurance for commercially awarded qualifications

criteria.text: The provider must ensure that each cohort of students is provided with an appropriately qualified and sufficient staff team for their course.
criteria.threshold: Staff team must have appropriate qualifications, sufficient numbers, and collective expertise for the course
risk_indicators: Staff team comprised solely of inexperienced teachers
Staff team with narrow collective expertise that doesn't cover the full range of student options
Over-reliance on visiting teachers to deliver large or significant elements of courses
Research supervision provided by staff who do not conduct research at the forefront of relevant disciplines

criteria.text: The provider must ensure that each cohort of students is provided with sufficient physical and digital learning resources for the purpose of ensuring a high quality academic experience and success in and beyond higher education.
criteria.threshold: Adequate physical facilities, laboratory resources, digital infrastructure, hardware, software, and internet access must be provided
risk_indicators: Students lack reliable access to required hardware or software
Technical infrastructure failures that are not promptly repaired
Inadequate laboratory, studio, or technical resources for subject requirements
Poor internet connectivity that disadvantages students relative to their peers

criteria.text: The provider must ensure that each cohort of students receives support that is sufficient to underpin success in and beyond higher education, including in relation to their physical and digital learning and teaching, and relating to avoiding academic misconduct.
criteria.threshold: Comprehensive academic support, digital learning support, and academic misconduct prevention must be provided
risk_indicators: Absence of academic support to help students with course content or address knowledge gaps
Lack of support to help students make effective use of digital learning resources
Insufficient guidance on essay planning, referencing, and consequences of academic misconduct
Inadequate careers support and guidance for future study or employment decisions

criteria.text: The provider must ensure that students are engaged, individually and collectively, in the quality of their educational experience.
criteria.threshold: Students must have meaningful opportunities to contribute to academic governance and provide feedback on their courses
risk_indicators: Absence of student membership or effective contribution to academic governance committees
Students not given range of opportunities to provide individual or collective feedback
No student representation on learning and teaching committees or course-level committees
Course changes made in response to feedback that compromise academic rigour

conditions.notes: The condition emphasizes that 'all reasonable steps' should prioritize student academic needs based on prior attainment and capability, with financial constraints given less weight. The document appears to continue with definitions in B2.4 but the text is cut off.

conditions.title: Assessment and awards

criteria.text: This condition applies to the quality of higher education provided in any manner or form by, or on behalf of, a provider (including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider is responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider).
criteria.threshold: Condition applies to all forms of higher education provision
risk_indicators: Provider claims exemption from assessment requirements for certain provision types
Unclear governance arrangements for awards granted on behalf of other providers
Different assessment standards applied across different modes of delivery

criteria.text: students are assessed effectively
criteria.threshold: All students receive effective assessment
risk_indicators: High rates of student complaints about assessment processes
Inconsistent assessment practices across courses or departments
Evidence of assessment methods not aligned with learning outcomes
Poor external examiner feedback on assessment design

criteria.text: each assessment is valid and reliable
criteria.threshold: All assessments demonstrate validity and reliability
risk_indicators: Significant variations in marking between assessors for similar work
Assessment methods that do not measure intended learning outcomes
Lack of moderation or quality assurance processes for assessments
External examiner concerns about assessment validity

criteria.text: academic regulations are designed to ensure that relevant awards are credible
criteria.threshold: Academic regulations ensure award credibility
risk_indicators: Academic regulations that are outdated or not aligned with sector standards
Inconsistent application of academic regulations across the institution
External concerns about the credibility of awards granted
Lack of clear academic standards in regulations

criteria.text: subject to paragraph B4.3, in respect of each higher education course, academic regulations are designed to ensure the effective assessment of technical proficiency in the English language in a manner which appropriately reflects the level and content of the applicable higher education course
criteria.threshold: English language proficiency effectively assessed for all courses (subject to exemptions)
risk_indicators: Graduates demonstrating inadequate English language skills for their qualification level
Absence of English language assessment requirements in academic regulations
English language assessment not calibrated to course level and content
Student complaints about language barriers affecting academic progression

criteria.text: relevant awards granted to students are credible at the point of being granted and when compared to those granted previously
criteria.threshold: Awards maintain credibility both currently and historically
risk_indicators: Significant unexplained variations in award outcomes over time
External examiner concerns about standards drift
Employer or sector feedback questioning award credibility
Statistical anomalies in degree classification distributions compared to sector norms

criteria.text: "academic misconduct" means any action or attempted action that may result in a student obtaining an unfair academic advantage in relation to an assessment, including but not limited to plagiarism, unauthorised collaboration and the possession of unauthorised materials during an assessment.
criteria.threshold: Clear definition and prevention of academic misconduct
risk_indicators: High rates of plagiarism detection in student submissions
Inadequate procedures for detecting unauthorized collaboration
Possession of unauthorized materials during assessments not properly monitored
Lack of clear academic misconduct policies communicated to students

criteria.text: "academic regulations" means regulations adopted by the provider, which govern its higher education courses, including but not limited to: i. the assessment of students' work; ii. student discipline relating to academic matters; iii. the requirements for relevant awards; and iv. the method used to determine classifications, including but not limited to: A. the requirements for an award; and B. the algorithms used to calculate the classification of awards.
criteria.threshold: Comprehensive academic regulations covering assessment, discipline, awards and classifications
risk_indicators: Absence of clear academic regulations or policies
Inconsistent application of assessment criteria across courses
Unclear or missing award requirements documentation
Opaque or undocumented classification calculation methods

criteria.text: "assessed effectively" means assessed in a challenging and appropriately comprehensive way, by reference to the subject matter of the higher education course, and includes but is not limited to: i. providing stretch and rigour consistent with the level of the course; ii. testing relevant skills; and iii. assessments being designed in a way that minimises the opportunities for academic misconduct and facilitates the detection of such misconduct where it does occur.
criteria.threshold: Assessments must be challenging, comprehensive, and designed to prevent and detect misconduct
risk_indicators: Assessments lacking appropriate academic rigor for course level
Limited variety in assessment methods testing only basic knowledge
Assessment designs that create easy opportunities for misconduct
Failure to detect obvious cases of academic misconduct

criteria.text: "credible" means that, in the reasonable opinion of the OfS, relevant awards reflect students' knowledge and skills, and for this purpose the OfS may take into account factors which include, but are not limited to: i. the number of relevant awards granted, and the classifications attached to them, and the way in which this number and/or the classifications change over time and compare with other providers; ii. whether students are assessed effectively and whether assessments are valid and reliable; iii. any actions the provider has taken that would result in an increased number of relevant awards, and/or changes in the classifications attached to them, whether or not the achievement of students has increased, for example, changes to assessment practices or academic regulations; and iv. the provider's explanation and evidence in support of the reasons for any changes in the classifications over time or differences with other providers.
criteria.threshold: Awards must credibly reflect student knowledge and skills as evidenced by comparative data and assessment practices
risk_indicators: Sudden unexplained increases in award numbers or grade classifications
Significant divergence from sector norms without justification
Changes to assessment practices that inflate outcomes without improved student achievement
Inability to provide credible evidence for grade distribution changes

criteria.text: Higher education provided "in any manner or form" includes any higher education course (whether or not that course is recognised for OfS funding purposes, or any other purpose), at any level, and with any volume of learning. This means, for example, that postgraduate research courses, the study of modules or courses leading to microcredentials, and apprenticeships are included within the scope of this condition. It also includes courses provided face-to-face, by distance learning, or a combination of delivery approaches.
criteria.threshold: All forms of higher education provision must be included within scope regardless of format, level, or recognition status
risk_indicators: Provider excludes certain course types from quality assurance processes
Different standards applied to online versus face-to-face delivery
Microcredentials or apprenticeships treated as outside regulatory scope
Inadequate oversight of non-traditional delivery methods

criteria.text: This condition applies to any higher education provided "by, or on behalf of, a provider". This includes higher education provided to all of the students who are registered with a registered provider, taught by a registered provider or studying for an award of a registered provider (or where these services are provided on a registered provider's behalf). This includes UK-based and non-UK-based students, and courses delivered through partnership arrangements both within the UK and internationally.
criteria.threshold: All higher education provision delivered by or on behalf of the provider must comply, including all students and partnership arrangements
risk_indicators: International partnerships operating under different quality standards
Third-party delivery arrangements lacking proper oversight
Different treatment of UK versus international students
Partnership agreements that dilute provider responsibility

criteria.text: The reference to "including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider is responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider" means that a provider is required to comply with the provisions of this condition where it is the awarding body for a course, whether or not that provider has any other role in the design or delivery of that course.
criteria.threshold: Awarding body responsibility applies even when provider has no other involvement in course design or delivery
risk_indicators: Awarding body lacks knowledge of course content or delivery standards
Minimal due diligence on teaching providers
Awards granted without proper quality verification
Unclear accountability between awarding and teaching bodies

criteria.text: Where a provider is not the awarding body for a course, this condition applies to a course the provider itself delivers, or which is delivered on its behalf, regardless of the identity of the awarding body, whether or not that awarding body is registered with the OfS, or the nature of any partnership agreement.
criteria.threshold: Teaching providers must comply regardless of awarding body identity or registration status
risk_indicators: Provider assumes external awarding body ensures compliance
Lack of internal quality processes for externally awarded courses
Different standards for courses with different awarding bodies
Inadequate monitoring of courses delivered on provider's behalf

criteria.text: Academic misconduct includes presenting work for assessment that is not the work of the student being assessed and includes but is not limited to the use of services offered by an essay mill.
criteria.threshold: Prevention and detection of academic misconduct including essay mill usage
risk_indicators: Students submitting work that is not their own
Evidence of essay mill usage by students
Lack of plagiarism detection systems or procedures
Absence of academic misconduct policies or enforcement

criteria.text: Students must be assessed effectively, including through differentiated assessment criteria, comprehensive subject coverage, compliant apprenticeship assessments, PSRB requirement adherence, timely feedback provision, and prevention of academic misconduct.
criteria.threshold: Effective assessment practices across all aspects of course delivery
risk_indicators: Same assessment criteria applied to students at different academic levels
Limited range of subject matter or skills being assessed
Apprenticeship assessments not meeting EPA requirements
Feedback not provided before final assessments or returned too late for learning

criteria.text: Assessments must be valid and reliable, testing what they are designed to assess and producing consistent results for equivalent achievement levels.
criteria.threshold: Valid and reliable assessment design and implementation
risk_indicators: Assessments focusing only on end-of-course material in long courses
Different marks awarded for equivalent levels of achievement
Assessment methods not aligned with stated learning outcomes
Inconsistent marking standards across similar assessments

criteria.text: Qualifications must be credible, with any increases in degree classifications or awards properly evidenced and justified, and research degrees awarded based on individual contribution to knowledge.
criteria.threshold: Credible qualifications with justified award patterns and standards
risk_indicators: Unexplained increases in first class or 2:1 degree proportions
Changes to degree classification algorithms without evidence of maintained standards
Research degrees awarded based on group contribution rather than individual knowledge
Lack of pre-emptive evidence and evaluation of changes to academic regulations

criteria.text: A provider must ensure that students who are awarded qualifications have the English language proficiency necessary for the qualification that they are to be awarded.
criteria.threshold: Students must demonstrate English language proficiency appropriate to their qualification level
risk_indicators: Students graduating without adequate English language skills for their qualification level
Assessment methods that do not effectively test English language competency
High numbers of students struggling with English language requirements during their studies
External examiner concerns about graduates' English language proficiency

criteria.text: A provider must only award qualifications to students who have demonstrated the English language proficiency necessary for the qualification through appropriate assessment.
criteria.threshold: Appropriate assessment methods must verify English language proficiency before qualification award
risk_indicators: Lack of robust English language assessment procedures
Qualifications awarded to students who have not undergone appropriate English proficiency testing
Assessment methods that fail to adequately measure English language competency
Inconsistent application of English language requirements across different courses

criteria.text: The requirements in B4.1 and B4.2 do not apply to the extent that a provider can demonstrate that compliance with them would conflict with the Equality Act 2010.
criteria.threshold: Compelling evidence and legal reasoning must demonstrate actual conflict with Equality Act 2010
risk_indicators: Over-broad application of Equality Act exceptions without specific legal justification
General claims about equality concerns rather than particular course-specific conflicts
Inability to provide compelling evidence of legal conflict with Equality Act 2010
Use of exception beyond what is strictly necessary to avoid discrimination

criteria.text: The provider must ensure that all of the work of an individual student can be identified from the records.
criteria.threshold: Complete identification of individual student work from institutional records
risk_indicators: Incomplete or missing assessment records for individual students
Unable to trace specific pieces of work back to individual students
Assessment recording systems lack unique student identifiers
Anonymous or pooled assessment records that cannot be disaggregated by student

criteria.text: Where students' personal data cannot be anonymised, the provider must retain this data for five years after the end date of a course.
criteria.threshold: Five-year retention period for non-anonymised student personal data post-course completion
risk_indicators: Student records deleted before five-year retention period expires
Data retention policies shorter than required five-year minimum
Missing or incomplete student data from courses completed within past five years
No systematic process for tracking data retention timelines

conditions.notes: This condition includes exemptions for courses assessing non-English languages and circumstances where English language assessment requirements would constitute unlawful discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. The document appears to contain definitions section B4.4 which is cut off in the provided text.

conditions.title: Sector-recognised standards

criteria.text: This condition relates to the standards applied to higher education provided in any manner or form by, or on behalf of, a provider (including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider is responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider).
criteria.threshold: Apply condition to all higher education provision regardless of delivery method or awarding arrangements
risk_indicators: Provider unclear about scope of condition across different delivery modes
Inconsistent application of standards between directly delivered and partnership provision
Confusion about responsibilities when acting as awarding body only

criteria.text: Without prejudice to the principles and requirements provided for by any other condition of registration and the scope of B5.1, the provider must ensure that, in respect of any relevant awards granted to students who complete a higher education course provided by, or on behalf of, the provider (whether or not the provider is the awarding body): a. any standards set appropriately reflect any applicable sector-recognised standards; and b. awards are only granted to students whose knowledge and skills appropriately reflect any applicable sector-recognised standards.
criteria.threshold: Ensure all awards reflect applicable sector-recognised standards in both standard-setting and student achievement
risk_indicators: Awards granted to students who have not met sector-recognised standards
Course standards that do not align with relevant sector-recognised benchmarks
Inconsistent application of sector standards across different programmes
External examiner or professional body concerns about standard alignment

criteria.text: "higher education course" is to be interpreted: i. in accordance with the Higher Education and Research Act 2017; and ii. so as to include, for the avoidance of doubt: A. a course of study; B. a programme of research; C. any further education course that forms an integrated part of a higher education course; and D. any module that forms part of a higher education course, whether or not that module is delivered as an integrated part of the course.
criteria.threshold: Proper interpretation and application of higher education course definition in accordance with HERA 2017
risk_indicators: Misclassification of courses as higher education when they do not meet statutory definition
Failure to include integrated further education components in higher education course delivery
Inconsistent application of course definitions across different programmes
Regulatory non-compliance due to incorrect course categorisation

criteria.text: "relevant award" means: i. a research award; ii. a taught award; and/or iii. any other type of award or qualification in respect of a higher education course, including an award of credit granted in respect of a module that may form part of a larger higher education course, whether or not granted pursuant to an authorisation given by or under the Higher Education and Research Act 2017, another Act of Parliament or Royal Charter.
criteria.threshold: Correct identification and treatment of all types of relevant awards including research, taught, and other qualifications
risk_indicators: Awards granted that do not align with the statutory definition of relevant awards
Failure to recognise modular credit awards as relevant awards
Inconsistent treatment of awards across different authorisation frameworks
Awarding qualifications outside the scope of relevant award definitions

criteria.text: "research award" and "taught award" have the meanings given in section 42(3) of the Higher Education and Research Act 2017.
criteria.threshold: Adherence to statutory definitions of research and taught awards as specified in HERA 2017
risk_indicators: Misclassification of awards as research or taught contrary to statutory definitions
Failure to apply section 42(3) HERA definitions consistently
Awards that do not meet the statutory criteria for their designated category
Regulatory discrepancies in award classification and reporting

criteria.text: "sector-recognised standards" means the standards contained in the document of that title published by the OfS from time to time.
criteria.threshold: Application of current sector-recognised standards as published and updated by OfS
risk_indicators: Use of outdated versions of sector-recognised standards
Failure to implement updated standards published by OfS
Non-alignment with current OfS sector-recognised standards documentation
Inconsistent application of sector standards across institutional activities

criteria.text: The reference to higher education provided "in any manner or form" includes any higher education course (whether or not that course is recognised for OfS funding purposes, or any other purpose), at any level, and with any volume of learning.
criteria.threshold: All higher education courses regardless of recognition, level, or volume must comply
risk_indicators: Provider excludes certain course types or levels from quality assurance processes
Different standards applied to microcredentials, apprenticeships, or postgraduate research
Inconsistent oversight of face-to-face versus distance learning provision
Provider claims certain courses are outside regulatory scope

criteria.text: This condition applies to any higher education provided "by, or on behalf of, a provider". This includes higher education provided to all of the students who are registered with a registered provider, taught by a registered provider or studying for an award of a registered provider (or where these services are provided on a registered provider's behalf).
criteria.threshold: Compliance required for all students and courses regardless of location or delivery arrangement
risk_indicators: Different quality standards applied to UK versus international students
Partnership arrangements lack adequate oversight or monitoring
Provider disclaims responsibility for courses delivered by partners
Inconsistent application of policies across different student populations

criteria.text: The reference to "including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider is responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider" means that a provider is required to comply with the provisions of this condition where it is the awarding body for a course, whether or not that provider has any other role in the design or delivery of that course.
criteria.threshold: Awarding bodies must ensure compliance even when not involved in course design or delivery
risk_indicators: Awarding body lacks oversight of courses for which it grants awards
Minimal due diligence on teaching providers using the awarding body's qualifications
Weak validation or approval processes for externally delivered courses
Provider claims no responsibility for quality of courses it does not directly deliver

criteria.text: Where a provider is not the awarding body for a course, this condition applies to a course the provider itself delivers, or which is delivered on its behalf, regardless of the identity of the awarding body, whether or not that awarding body is registered with the OfS, or the nature of any partnership agreement.
criteria.threshold: Teaching providers must ensure compliance for all courses delivered, regardless of awarding body
risk_indicators: Provider assumes external awarding body ensures all compliance requirements
Different quality assurance applied to courses with non-OfS registered awarding bodies
Lack of oversight for courses leading to qualifications from commercial awarding bodies
Partnership agreements that disclaim quality responsibilities

criteria.text: "Relevant award" includes, but is not limited to, the awards made by a provider in relation to an apprenticeship.
criteria.threshold: Provider must understand scope of relevant awards including apprenticeship awards
risk_indicators: Provider excludes apprenticeship awards from quality assurance processes
Inconsistent treatment of different award types
Unclear definition of what constitutes a 'relevant award'

criteria.text: Where monitoring activity produces intelligence or evidence that suggests there may be compliance concerns for an individual provider, the OfS may adopt one or more of the following approaches in any order: a. Engage with a provider to ensure it is aware of the issues. b. Gather further information it considers relevant to the scope of the potential concerns, from a provider or from elsewhere on a voluntary basis, to facilitate an assessment of whether there is, or has been, a breach of one or more conditions. c. Use its investigatory powers where that is considered appropriate for any reason.
criteria.threshold: Provider must cooperate with OfS monitoring and investigation processes
risk_indicators: Non-response or delayed response to OfS engagement
Refusal to provide requested information voluntarily
Obstructive behaviour during investigations
Failure to acknowledge or address identified issues

criteria.text: A provider is therefore expected to retain appropriate records of students' assessed work for such regulatory purposes for a period of five years after the end date of a course. Where possible, a provider is expected to retain records of student assessments in an anonymised form by removing students' personal data from the records. However, in doing so, it should ensure that removal of students' personal data would not limit the OfS's ability to assess the provider's compliance.
criteria.threshold: Provider must retain student assessment records for five years post-course completion in accessible form for regulatory review
risk_indicators: Systematic deletion of student work before five-year retention period
Inability to produce assessment records when requested
Over-anonymisation that prevents effective compliance assessment
Poor record-keeping systems that compromise accessibility

criteria.text: The provider must retain records of students' assessed work, including by ensuring that all of the work of an individual student can be identified from the records.
criteria.threshold: Complete retention of identifiable assessed work records for all students
risk_indicators: Missing or incomplete assessment records for individual students
Inability to identify specific student work from retained records
Systematic gaps in assessment documentation
Records that cannot be linked to individual student identities

criteria.text: Where students' personal data cannot be anonymised, the provider must retain this data for five years after the end date of a course.
criteria.threshold: Five-year retention period for non-anonymised student personal data post-course completion
risk_indicators: Deletion of student records before the five-year minimum period
Incomplete data retention policies or procedures
Evidence of premature data destruction
Inability to produce records within the required retention timeframe

criteria.text: the standards to be set for a provider's courses appropriately reflect sector-recognised standards
criteria.threshold: Standards for courses must appropriately reflect sector-recognised standards
risk_indicators: Course standards significantly below comparable sector benchmarks
Absence of reference to sector-recognised frameworks in course design
External examiner reports highlighting standards concerns
Graduation outcomes inconsistent with sector norms

criteria.text: the awards to be granted for those courses will only be granted to students with knowledge and skills which also appropriately reflect sector-recognised standards
criteria.threshold: Awards only granted to students demonstrating knowledge and skills reflecting sector-recognised standards
risk_indicators: High pass rates without corresponding sector-level competencies
Graduate employer feedback indicating skills gaps
Assessment practices not aligned with sector standards
Lack of evidence of graduate achievement of learning outcomes

conditions.notes: Condition applies to all higher education provision including partnership arrangements. Provider must ensure sector-recognised standards are reflected in both the setting of standards and the granting of awards. Document appears to be incomplete as definitions section (B5.3) is cut off.

conditions.title: Quality

criteria.text: This condition applies to the quality of higher education to be provided in any manner or form by, or on behalf of, a provider (including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider would be responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider).
criteria.threshold: Condition applies to all forms of higher education provision
risk_indicators: Provider unclear about scope of their higher education provision
Inconsistent quality standards across different delivery modes
Lack of oversight of education delivered on behalf of the provider
Confusion about responsibility for award-granting partnerships

criteria.text: Without prejudice to the principles and requirements provided for by any other condition of registration and the scope of B7.1, the provider must: a. have credible plans that would enable the provider, if registered, to comply with conditions B1, B2 and B4 from the date of registration; and b. have the capacity and resources necessary to deliver, in practice, those plans.
criteria.threshold: Credible compliance plans for conditions B1, B2, B4 plus adequate capacity and resources to deliver them
risk_indicators: Vague or unrealistic compliance plans lacking specific detail
Insufficient staffing or expertise to meet quality requirements
Inadequate financial resources allocated to quality assurance
Plans that cannot be implemented by registration date

criteria.text: "capacity and resources" includes, but is not limited to: the financial resources of the provider; the number, expertise, and experience of the staff employed, and to be employed, by the provider; the physical and digital learning resources deployed, and to be deployed, by the provider and the provider's management and governance arrangements.
criteria.threshold: Demonstrate adequate financial resources, staffing expertise, learning resources, and management arrangements
risk_indicators: Financial statements showing declining reserves or cash flow problems
High staff turnover or recruitment difficulties in key academic positions
Outdated or insufficient physical/digital learning infrastructure
Weak management structures or governance failures

criteria.text: "credible" includes, but is not limited to, evidence of the provider's past performance delivering higher education.
criteria.threshold: Provide evidence of successful track record in higher education delivery
risk_indicators: Limited or no previous experience in higher education provision
Poor performance indicators from previous educational activities
Lack of documented evidence of successful program delivery
Negative feedback from students or external quality assessments

criteria.text: This condition applies to the courses that the provider plans to provide when it is registered.
criteria.threshold: Must apply to all planned courses upon registration
risk_indicators: Provider fails to identify or list all courses they plan to offer
Missing coverage of certain course types in compliance documentation
Unclear scope of courses covered by quality assurance processes

criteria.text: The reference to higher education provided "in any manner or form" includes any higher education course (whether or not that course is recognised for OfS funding purposes, or any other purpose), at any level, and with any volume of learning. This means, for example, that postgraduate research courses, the study of modules or courses leading to microcredentials, and apprenticeships are included within the scope of this condition. It also includes courses provided face-to-face, by distance learning, or a combination of delivery approaches.
criteria.threshold: Must cover all forms of higher education delivery regardless of format, level, or recognition status
risk_indicators: Quality processes exclude certain delivery modes like distance learning
Different standards applied to micro-credentials or apprenticeships
Inadequate oversight of blended or online course components
Missing policies for non-traditional course formats

criteria.text: This condition applies to any higher education provided "by, or on behalf of, a provider". This includes higher education provided to all of the students who are registered with a registered provider, taught by a registered provider or studying for an award of a registered provider (or where these services are provided on a registered provider's behalf). This includes UK-based and non-UK-based students, and courses delivered through partnership arrangements both within the UK and internationally.
criteria.threshold: Must ensure compliance extends to all educational provision including partnerships and international delivery
risk_indicators: Inadequate quality oversight of partnership arrangements
Different standards for international vs domestic provision
Poor monitoring of courses delivered on provider's behalf
Gaps in student support for non-UK based learners

criteria.text: The reference to "including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider would be responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider" means that a provider is required to comply with the provisions of this condition where it would be the awarding body for a course, whether or not that provider would have any other role in the design or delivery of that course.
criteria.threshold: Must maintain compliance responsibility when acting solely as awarding body
risk_indicators: Insufficient due diligence on courses for which awards are granted
Lack of quality assurance processes for award-only arrangements
Missing oversight of academic standards where not involved in delivery
Inadequate verification of student achievement in award-only cases

criteria.text: The OfS is likely to draw on evidence submitted by a provider as part of its application for registration to make a judgement about whether the provider has "credible" plans in accordance with this requirement. For example, evidence submitted about the policies and processes a provider proposes to have in place to ensure compliance with ongoing conditions B1, B2 and B4, and evidence of how the provider intends to assess its own compliance with those conditions, is likely to be relevant.
criteria.threshold: Provider must submit credible evidence of policies, processes and compliance assessment methods for ongoing conditions
risk_indicators: Vague or incomplete policy documentation submitted during registration
Lack of specific compliance monitoring procedures outlined in application
Absence of clear assessment frameworks for ongoing condition compliance
Generic rather than tailored compliance plans that don't address provider's specific context

criteria.text: Where a provider seeking registration has previously delivered, or is currently delivering, higher education courses, the "plans" required under this condition may include evidence relating to the provider's experience of delivery of those courses. The OfS will have regard to that evidence in determining whether the condition is satisfied.
criteria.threshold: Provider must provide evidence of previous or current higher education course delivery experience where applicable
risk_indicators: Poor student outcomes or quality issues in previously delivered courses
Inability to demonstrate successful track record of course delivery
Significant gaps or inconsistencies in delivery experience evidence
Previous regulatory concerns or interventions at other institutions involving key staff

criteria.text: Where a registered provider is seeking registration in a different category of registration, this requires the OfS to deregister the provider and make a new registration decision by deciding whether each of the initial conditions is satisfied. In these circumstances "date of registration" means the date of the new registration.
criteria.threshold: Provider changing registration category must satisfy all initial conditions as if applying for new registration
risk_indicators: Previous compliance issues in current registration category that may affect new category application
Inadequate preparation for requirements specific to the new registration category
Failure to demonstrate capacity to meet enhanced obligations of higher registration categories
Timing issues that create regulatory gaps during transition between categories

criteria.text: The OfS is likely to draw on evidence submitted by a provider as part of its application for registration to make a judgement about whether the provider has the "capacity and resources" necessary to deliver, in practice, its plans. For example, evidence submitted in relation to a provider's financial viability and sustainability, or its management and governance arrangements are likely to be relevant.
criteria.threshold: Demonstrate capacity and resources necessary to deliver plans in practice through application evidence
risk_indicators: Insufficient financial resources relative to planned activities
Weak management and governance structures for proposed scale of operations
Gap between ambitious plans and available capacity to deliver
Lack of evidence of sustainable resource planning

criteria.text: The OfS will assess compliance with this initial condition for all providers seeking registration. It may commission the designated quality body to conduct an assessment of quality to provide information to the OfS to inform the OfS's decision about whether the condition is satisfied. An assessment by the designated quality body will involve the submission of specified information to the designated quality body and will normally involve a visit to the provider and interviews with relevant staff and students.
criteria.threshold: Successfully complete OfS compliance assessment process including potential designated quality body assessment
risk_indicators: Failure to provide required information to designated quality body
Poor performance during site visits or staff/student interviews
Inability to demonstrate quality arrangements in practice
Significant concerns raised during assessment visits

criteria.text: Where a provider, or another legal entity that the OfS considers to be operating substantially the same higher education business, has previously been registered, a history of non-compliance with ongoing conditions B1, B2 or B4 is likely to result in a judgement that initial
criteria.threshold: Demonstrate compliance history with ongoing conditions B1, B2 or B4 if previously registered
risk_indicators: Previous breaches of conditions B1, B2 or B4 by provider or related entity
Pattern of non-compliance in previous registration
Unresolved quality or standards issues from previous registration
Same management responsible for previous compliance failures

criteria.text: Where the OfS considers this initial condition to be satisfied, but that there is an increased risk of a breach of one or more of the general ongoing conditions for quality (conditions B1, B2 and B4), or a wider regulatory concern, it may impose one or more specific ongoing conditions of registration and will also consider whether additional monitoring requirements are appropriate, for example, a requirement to report additional matters as reportable events, or to have an additional assessment by the OfS, the designated quality body, or other appropriate body.
criteria.threshold: Initial quality standards must be demonstrated before ongoing registration conditions apply
risk_indicators: Deficiencies identified during initial quality assessment that indicate potential future compliance issues
Quality management systems that are underdeveloped or lack robust processes
Evidence of quality concerns in academic delivery or student outcomes during initial assessment
Insufficient evidence of institutional capacity to maintain ongoing quality standards

conditions.notes: This condition focuses on ensuring providers have credible plans and adequate capacity to meet core quality conditions (B1, B2, B4) from registration. The scope covers all forms of higher education provision including partnership arrangements. Document appears incomplete with definitions section cut off.

conditions.title: Standards

criteria.text: This condition relates to the standards to be applied to higher education to be provided in any manner or form by, or on behalf of, a provider (including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider would be responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider).
criteria.threshold: Define scope of standards application across all forms of higher education provision
risk_indicators: Provider fails to identify all forms of education delivery requiring standards oversight
Unclear delineation of responsibility for standards in partnership arrangements
Lack of documented scope for standards application across different provision models

criteria.text: Without prejudice to the principles and requirements provided for by any other condition of registration and the scope of B8.1, the provider must demonstrate, in a credible manner, that any standards to be set and/or applied in respect of any relevant awards granted to students who complete a higher education course provided by, or on behalf of, the provider (if registered), whether or not the provider is the awarding body, appropriately reflect any applicable sector-recognised standards.
criteria.threshold: Credibly demonstrate that award standards appropriately reflect applicable sector-recognised standards
risk_indicators: Awards granted that do not align with established sector benchmarks or frameworks
Absence of clear mapping between course standards and sector-recognised standards
Inadequate evidence or documentation supporting standards alignment claims
External examiner or quality assurance reports highlighting standards concerns

criteria.text: "credible" includes, but is not limited to, evidence of the provider's past performance delivering higher education.
criteria.threshold: Provider must demonstrate credible evidence including past performance in delivering higher education
risk_indicators: Provider has no documented history of delivering higher education
Previous higher education delivery resulted in poor outcomes or regulatory breaches
Evidence of performance in higher education delivery is unsubstantiated or lacking credibility

criteria.text: "higher education course" is to be interpreted: i. in accordance with the Higher Education and Research Act 2017; and ii. so as to include, for the avoidance of doubt: A. a course of study; B. a programme of research; C. any further education course that forms an integrated part of a higher education course; and D. any module that forms part of a higher education course, whether or not that module is delivered as an integrated part of the course.
criteria.threshold: Provider must apply the correct definition of higher education course as specified
risk_indicators: Provider incorrectly categorizes courses as higher education when they do not meet HERA 2017 definitions
Provider fails to include integrated further education components in higher education course definitions
Provider misclassifies standalone modules or research programmes

criteria.text: "relevant award" means: i. a research award; ii. a taught award; and/or iii. any other type of award or qualification in respect of a higher education course, including an award of credit granted in respect of a module that may form part of a larger higher education course, whether or not granted pursuant to an authorisation given by or under the Higher Education and Research Act 2017, another Act of Parliament or Royal Charter.
criteria.threshold: Provider must correctly identify and categorize all relevant awards within scope
risk_indicators: Provider fails to recognize module credits as relevant awards
Provider excludes awards not granted under HERA 2017 from relevant award category
Provider misclassifies research or taught awards

criteria.text: "research award" and "taught award" have the meanings given in section 42(3) of the Higher Education and Research Act 2017.
criteria.threshold: Provider must apply HERA 2017 section 42(3) definitions for research and taught awards
risk_indicators: Provider uses definitions for research and taught awards that contradict HERA 2017 section 42(3)
Provider fails to distinguish properly between research and taught award categories

criteria.text: "sector-recognised standards" means the standards contained in the document of that title published by the OfS from time to time, and which apply as at the date of the provider's application for registration.
criteria.threshold: Provider must reference current OfS sector-recognised standards as at application date
risk_indicators: Provider references outdated or superseded versions of sector-recognised standards
Provider fails to identify which version of standards applies to their application
Provider uses non-OfS standards documents when referencing sector-recognised standards

criteria.text: This condition applies to the courses that the provider plans to provide when it is registered.
criteria.threshold: Provider must ensure condition applies to all planned courses upon registration
risk_indicators: Provider has unclear or incomplete course planning documentation
Significant changes to course portfolio after registration without proper assessment
Provider fails to identify which courses fall under this condition's scope

criteria.text: The reference to higher education provided "in any manner or form" includes any higher education course (whether or not that course is recognised for OfS funding purposes, or any other purpose), at any level, and with any volume of learning.
criteria.threshold: Provider must apply condition to all forms of higher education delivery including postgraduate research, microcredentials, apprenticeships, face-to-face, distance learning, and combined approaches
risk_indicators: Provider excludes certain course types from quality assurance processes
Different standards applied to funded versus non-funded courses
Inconsistent quality measures across different delivery modes
Provider treats microcredentials or apprenticeships as outside regulatory scope

criteria.text: This condition applies to any higher education provided "by, or on behalf of, a provider". This includes higher education provided to all of the students who are registered with a registered provider, taught by a registered provider or studying for an award of a registered provider (or where these services are provided on a registered provider's behalf). This includes UK-based and non-UK-based students, and courses delivered through partnership arrangements both within the UK and internationally.
criteria.threshold: Provider must ensure condition compliance for all students and delivery arrangements including partnerships and international provision
risk_indicators: Weaker oversight of partner-delivered courses compared to directly delivered courses
Different quality standards applied to international versus domestic provision
Inadequate monitoring of courses delivered on provider's behalf
Poor communication of standards to delivery partners

criteria.text: The reference to "including, but not limited to, circumstances where a provider would be responsible only for granting awards for students registered with another provider" means that a provider is required to comply with the provisions of this condition where it would be the awarding body for a course, whether or not that provider would have any other role in the design or delivery of that course.
criteria.threshold: Provider acting as awarding body must ensure condition compliance regardless of their role in course design or delivery
risk_indicators: Awarding body lacks adequate oversight of courses it validates but does not deliver
Insufficient quality assurance processes for award-only arrangements
Poor communication between awarding body and teaching provider about standards
Awarding body unable to demonstrate course quality despite granting awards

criteria.text: The provider will deliver courses that appropriately reflect sector-recognised standards
criteria.threshold: Standards set in courses must appropriately reflect sector-recognised standards as assessed by the designated quality body
risk_indicators: Courses failing to meet sector-recognised academic standards during designated quality body assessment
Inconsistent or unclear academic standards frameworks across programmes
Staff unable to articulate how course standards align with sector expectations during interviews
Assessment methods or learning outcomes that do not reflect appropriate academic level

criteria.text: The provider must satisfy the OfS that condition B8 is satisfied, following assessment by the designated quality body.
criteria.threshold: OfS determination that condition B8 requirements are met based on designated quality body assessment
risk_indicators: Designated quality body provides negative assessment or identifies significant concerns
Provider fails to engage adequately with designated quality body review process
History of non-compliance with ongoing conditions where provider or related entity was previously registered
OfS receives information suggesting provider cannot meet condition B8 requirements

criteria.text: Where the OfS considers this initial condition to be satisfied, but that there is an increased risk of a breach of the general ongoing condition for standards
criteria.threshold: Initial condition for standards must be satisfied with no increased risk of breach
risk_indicators: OfS assessment indicates increased risk of standards breach despite initial satisfaction
Evidence suggests potential future non-compliance with ongoing standards requirements
Institutional capacity or systems show vulnerabilities in maintaining standards
Previous performance indicators suggest declining standards trajectory

conditions.notes: Document appears incomplete, ending mid-sentence in definitions section B8.3. Condition covers standards application across all forms of higher education provision, including partnership arrangements where the provider may not be the awarding body.
